stories.title: WSJ: The AI Backlash Has Tech Executives Fearing for Their Lives
stories.paragraph: The Wall Street Journal reports that a growing number of technology executives involved in AI development have begun taking personal security precautions in response to threats linked to public opposition to the technology. The trend reflects an escalation in the social and political tensions surrounding AI deployment, moving beyond online criticism into physical intimidation. The report comes amid a broader pattern of anti-AI sentiment directed at prominent figures in the industry.

stories.title: EU officials peeved after Anthropic sends junior staffer to testify about safety
stories.paragraph: European Parliament officials expressed frustration after Anthropic sent a junior employee named Donny Greenberg to testify on AI safety matters, rather than a senior executive. The move was seen as a sign of disrespect toward the legislative body's oversight role, particularly given the gravity of AI safety discussions under the EU AI Act framework. The incident highlights ongoing friction between major AI companies and European regulators as they negotiate the terms of compliance and accountability.

stories.title: We don't use AI in any of our design or production processes
stories.paragraph: Design studio Mass-Driver published a public statement affirming that it does not use AI in any part of its design or production workflow. The post positions the decision as a values-driven choice centered on craft and human authorship, and serves as a form of brand differentiation in a market where AI-assisted design is increasingly common. The statement is notable as a concrete example of a creative business explicitly opting out of AI tooling and making that policy public-facing.

stories.title: xai-org/grok-build, now open source
stories.paragraph: xAI open-sourced its Grok CLI coding agent, but the release was immediately overshadowed by reports that running the tool in a directory caused it to upload the entire directory's contents to xAI's Google Cloud storage buckets. At least one user reported that this included sensitive files such as SSH keys, a password manager database, and personal media. Simon Willison documented the incident, raising concerns about the tool's data handling behavior and the risks of running AI coding agents with broad filesystem access.

stories.title: Mermaid to Unicode box art (grok-mermaid)
stories.paragraph: While examining the open-sourced Grok CLI codebase, Simon Willison discovered a Rust-based terminal renderer for Mermaid diagrams called grok-mermaid, located at xai-grok-markdown/src/mermaid.rs. He compiled it to WebAssembly and ran it in a browser to render Mermaid diagrams as Unicode box art, using Claude Code for web (Fable 5) to assist with the conversion. The experiment demonstrates a secondary use of components from the Grok CLI release independent of the privacy controversy surrounding the main tool.

stories.title: Open Source, Free Tier Capable Whispr Using Cloudflare AI
stories.paragraph: A developer published Voicebox, an open-source voice transcription tool that uses OpenAI's Whisper model via Cloudflare's AI platform, with a free tier available. The project is notable for leveraging Cloudflare's AI inference infrastructure to avoid requiring users to run local GPU hardware or pay per-API-call fees beyond the free tier. The repository is publicly available on GitHub and positions itself as a lightweight alternative for developers needing speech-to-text without significant infrastructure cost.

stories.title: Linus Torvalds tells AI haters to fork off
stories.paragraph: Linux creator Linus Torvalds publicly dismissed critics of AI within the open-source community, using characteristically blunt language to argue that opposition to AI tools is unproductive. His remarks, reported by The Register, position him as broadly supportive of AI adoption in software development contexts. The statement carries weight given Torvalds' long-standing authority in the open-source world and his history of outspoken opinions on development practices.

stories.title: Linus Torvalds "Linux is not one of those anti-AI projects"
stories.paragraph: In a message posted to the Linux kernel mailing list, Linus Torvalds stated directly that Linux is not an anti-AI project, clarifying the project's official stance amid community debate. The message was sent to the linux-media mailing list and represents a primary-source record of Torvalds' position, distinct from secondary media coverage. His statement leaves room for AI-generated or AI-assisted contributions to be considered on their technical merits rather than excluded on principle.

stories.title: Stop saying that AI is just a tool and it only matters how it is used
stories.paragraph: A blog post on frank.computer argues against the common framing that AI is neutral and that outcomes depend solely on user intent, a position often summarized as "it's just a tool." The author contends that this framing obscures how technologies embed values, incentives, and power structures that shape outcomes independent of individual use. The piece draws on philosophy of technology to challenge what it calls a reductive and potentially harmful rhetorical move in public discourse about AI.

stories.title: Generative AI is an Engineering Disaster
stories.paragraph: An Atlantic article argues that generative AI systems are producing serious problems in software engineering contexts, describing patterns of unreliable code generation, confidently wrong outputs, and maintenance burdens that accrue when AI-generated code is integrated into production systems. The piece is notable for focusing on engineering practice rather than broader societal concerns, making a technical case for skepticism about current generative AI capabilities in professional software development. It arrives amid a period of intense industry debate about the productivity gains versus risks of AI coding tools.

stories.title: Speculative Growth and the AI "Bubble" [pdf]
stories.paragraph: A working paper from MIT Economics examines whether current AI investment patterns constitute a speculative bubble, applying economic models of speculative growth to the AI sector. The paper distinguishes between investment driven by expected productive returns and investment sustained by expectations of resale gains, a classic framing for bubble analysis. Its origin at MIT's economics department gives it academic standing in what has become a contested empirical and theoretical debate about AI's macroeconomic trajectory.

stories.title: Governments, companies, nonprofits should invest in free, open source AI [pdf]
stories.paragraph: A paper published by the Siegel Endowment, authored by David Siegel, argues that governments, corporations, and nonprofits should direct investment toward free and open-source AI development rather than relying primarily on proprietary systems. The argument centers on concerns about concentration of AI capability in a small number of private companies and the risks this poses to public access and democratic accountability. The paper is positioned as a policy recommendation and represents an institutional voice calling for structural changes in how AI is funded and distributed.

stories.title: Deep Interaction: An Efficient Human-AI Interaction Method for Large Reasoning Models
stories.paragraph: Researchers have proposed Deep Interaction, a method designed to make human correction of large reasoning models more efficient during Chain-of-Thought (CoT) inference. Current approaches to fixing model errors are costly: either the model regenerates a full response that may repeat the same mistake, or users must manually identify the faulty reasoning step across multiple follow-up turns. Deep Interaction addresses this by enabling more targeted, mid-reasoning intervention, allowing users to correct errors at the specific step where they occur rather than restarting the entire generation. The work is notable because it directly targets the interaction overhead introduced by long-form CoT reasoning, which has become common in frontier models.
